All reports
Reverse Cavity
REVERSE CAVITY

Competitive Intelligence Report

The competitive battlefield, the exposures, and the lanes Reverse Cavity can take.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Reverse Cavity’s prized claim, that rivals “only repackage retail products” or “work from the outside in,” is half right and strategically dangerous to lead with. Dr. Ellie Phillips literally bundles Crest, Listerine, and ACT and admits it, which proves the repackaging charge against one rival.1 All three named competitors already market the “inside-out, works-with-your-body, root-cause minerals” thesis in their own copy, so that mechanism is category-standard, not a differentiator.2 3 4 The win lane is the most-proven, fully-guided expression of the category promise, anchored by the founder’s independently dentist-confirmed reversal and the fluoride-free ingestible-mineral position Phillips has ceded.5 6

Reverse Cavity competes against three credentialed and well-reviewed brands, and it ties or trails them on the dimensions buyers can see today. Phillips holds a DDS since 1970, an Andrew Huberman endorsement, tier-one media logos, and 1,511 store reviews.7 Dr. Michelle Jorgensen holds a DDS plus a Board Certified Traditional Naturopath credential, claims “1,000,000+ jars sold” and 3,400+ reviews, ships six books, and already runs the full product ecosystem Reverse Cavity only aspires to build.8 9 10 Bliss Energetics carries 563 reviews at roughly 4.42 out of 5 and a 30-plus-herb proprietary formula that no off-the-shelf repackager could assemble.11 12 On credential, catalog breadth, and review volume, Reverse Cavity lags all three.

The single biggest exposure sits in the competitors’ own copy. Each one already says “inside-out,” “works with your body,” “root cause,” and “remineralize from within.”2 3 4 13 A brand that leads with a borrowed sentence has no defensible position. The single biggest open lane is the combination only Reverse Cavity can assemble: a fluoride-free ingestible-mineral system (Phillips is 100% topical and pro-fluoride), one guided complete-protocol kit (Jorgensen’s catalog forces choice overload and her flagship bundle was out of stock at capture), and a founder reversal an independent third dentist put on the record.6 14 15 The rest of this report maps the battlefield that produces those two conclusions.

1. Competitor Matrix and Client Snapshot

Figure 1. Capability presence per competitor. Reverse Cavity owns the lane no credentialed rival holds: a guided, ingestible, fluoride-free kit. Use on sales calls when prospects ask “how do you compare to X?”

The dimensions below are the ones a fluoride-skeptical, repeat-cavity buyer actually weighs: who stands behind the product, what it costs to start, whether the “natural” claim survives an ingredient read, and whether the proof is the buyer’s own or the seller’s.

Dimension Dr. Ellie Phillips Dr. Michelle Jorgensen Bliss Energetics Reverse Cavity (client)
Founder authority DDS (1970), AAOSH co-founder, Huberman/NYT/Fox logos7 DDS + Board Certified Traditional Naturopath, ~17 yrs practice16 Reiki/Ayurveda founder, no clinical credential11 Non-clinician founder with a dentist-confirmed personal reversal5
Lead positioning “The Complete Mouth Care System”17 “Fluoride-Free Dental Care Made by a Dentist Who Refused to Compromise”9 “All-natural, energetically-balanced herbal products”11 “Reverse your cavities” remineralization kit
Fluoride stance Fluoride-FORWARD; defends Crest/Listerine/ACT14 Fluoride-free9 Fluoride-free18 Fluoride-free
Inside-out / root-cause claim Yes, verbatim2 Yes, verbatim3 Yes, verbatim4 Yes (the claim it treats as unique)
Format 5-product retail bundle + protocol + $199 course1 19 14-category catalog + supplements + courses10 À-la-carte herbal products, no single kit11 Single guided complete-protocol kit
Entry price $68 kit; $199 course19 $20.47 single items; $100.97 flagship bundle (out of stock at capture)10 14 $26 toothpaste; $40-$65 putty11 [client to confirm]
Review proof Kit 180; store-wide 1,5117 19 Sitewide 3,400+; flagship bundle “Based on 1 review”8 15 Tooth Puddy 563 at ~4.4212 ~2-3 dozen written plus founder X-rays5
Geographic reach Continental USA only on the kit6 US + Amazon + resellers10 US + Faire wholesale11 DTC, expandable internationally
Ingestible mineral system None; topical only6 Supplements within a sprawling catalog10 Herbal blends, not a guided ingestible protocol11 The packaged inside-out protocol

Client snapshot. Reverse Cavity sells one guided remineralization kit aimed at the frustrated repeat-cavity sufferer who distrusts conventional dentistry and big pharma and leans natural.20 Its claimed differentiators are the inside-out mineral mechanism, the founder’s documented before-and-after, and a purpose-driven origin story. Its visible strengths are a focused single-decision product, a fluoride-free clean-label stance, and one founder reversal that a third independent dentist confirmed on the record.5 Its visible weaknesses are a non-clinician founder against three credentialed rivals, a thin and largely first-party proof base, a hero claim (“reverse your cavities”) that FDA and FTC treat as an unapproved drug claim, and a single-channel growth history resting on one video.21 22

2. Per-Competitor Deep Dives

2.1 Dr. Ellie Phillips, DDS: the credentialed repackager who beat the client to its own vocabulary

Phillips runs drellie.com as an education hub, shop.zellies.com as the Shopify store, and learn.drellie.com as a Kajabi course. Her system page H1 reads “The Complete Mouth Care System,” promising to “eliminate plaque, resolve gingivitis, prevent cavities, heal periodontal disease and gum problems while supporting a healthy oral microbiome.”17 She describes the mechanism in language Reverse Cavity treats as its own: the system “works in harmony with your body to balance mouth biochemistry” and “allows minerals from saliva to be absorbed by teeth supporting a process of natural mineralization that strengthens enamel, helps heal cavities.”2

The repackaging charge lands squarely here. Phillips states in her own copy that the system is “a specific collection of oral care products that are commonly found in grocery stores.”1 The $68 kit contains a 16 oz CloSYS rinse, Crest Cavity Protection toothpaste, Listerine, an ACT sodium-fluoride rinse, an instruction booklet, and two jars of her own Zellie’s xylitol.1 Her differentiation is method, sequence, and timing, and she tells buyers they can source the parts elsewhere. Reverse Cavity is right that a leading “natural” competitor is functionally a re-bundler of retail SKUs.

Her durable moat is her personal brand, the asset an anonymous-founder startup cannot match. DDS since 1970 from Guy’s Hospital in London, AAOSH co-founder in 2010, two published books, “trusted by over 1 million people worldwide,” a quoted Andrew Huberman endorsement, and “featured in” logos for Huberman Lab, Fox News, the New York Times, the New York Post, and The Times of London.7 The Process Power she claims, the synergistic six-step sequence, is documented in a ten-dollar book and free videos, so it copies trivially and is not a true moat. Her distribution is genuine scale relative to a startup: own Shopify, Amazon, iHerb, and Zellie’s mints now at Target.com.19

Her target buyer overlaps Reverse Cavity’s directly: adults with recurring cavities, recession, and sensitivity who resent the treatment treadmill and ask “why hasn’t my dentist told me about this.”20 Her product ladder runs the $68 kit (Subscribe and Save 15%, free shipping over $75), the $199 Gum and Tooth Rebuild Program against a claimed “comparable value $3,000,” and books under twenty dollars.19 Visible strengths include deep review proof (kit 180 reviews, 81% five-star; store-wide 1,511, 85% five-star) and a content engine with a blog post dated June 11, 2026.7

The weaknesses are sharp and several are forensic. Her $199 course page renders an unedited Kajabi template placeholder reading “Awesome Hook Headline / Benefit Driven Sub Headline Here / Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet,” and a headline typo on the same page reads “The Complete Mouth Care Syste(TM) is the steps.”22 Her own kit reviews surface adverse experiences: Katy S. wrote “I started the protocol 2 week ago and ended up with my mouth sores on the roof of my mouth and my teeth have never felt so sensitive,” and a four-star reviewer wrote “I don’t think this is the secret sauce it’s made out to be.”23 The kit cannot ship outside the continental USA even via a mail forwarder, contradicting her global mission.6

Adjacent whitespace next to Phillips. Phillips is fluoride-forward and 100% topical, and she actively argues against the anti-fluoride camp on TikTok (“If you’re so against fluoride, why aren’t you giving up these foods then?”).14 That places her on the opposite side of Reverse Cavity’s fluoride-skeptical buyer and cedes three lanes outright: the true ingestible inside-out mineral system she does not sell, the fluoride-free clean-label buyer she alienates, and the international market her kit cannot reach.6 A rep selling against Phillips wins by naming that her “natural” system is built on Crest and ACT fluoride, then offering the fluoride-free ingestible alternative.

2.2 Dr. Michelle Jorgensen: the full ecosystem the client is still trying to build

Jorgensen sells through livingwellwithdrmichelle.com and anchors her authority in Total Care Dental and Wellness in American Fork, Utah. Her homepage H1 reads “Fluoride-Free Dental Care Made by a Dentist Who Refused to Compromise,” with the subhead “Dr. Michelle couldn’t find clean oral care products safe enough for her own family. So she formulated them herself. Trusted by over 1 million customers.”9 She holds a durable credential stack: Functional Integrative Dentist (DDS) and Board Certified Traditional Naturopath, roughly 17 years in practice, training other practitioners in the “Total Care Way.”16 This is a credentialed authority brand. The repackaging charge does not apply to her.

Her copy occupies Reverse Cavity’s core claim with more authority than the client can muster. Her bundle page is headed “Support Your Teeth From the Inside Out,” explaining that teeth are “living structures nourished by small blood vessels” and that “when we provide our body with the proper vitamins, minerals, and nutrients, we support overall tooth care.”3 She targets the identical wound: “if you find yourself frequently getting cavities despite proper oral care, it may be due to a lack of internal nourishment,” developed “to support the body’s natural ability to heal and protect itself.”25 A credentialed DDS published the inside-out systemic-mineral thesis Reverse Cavity treats as proprietary.

Her social proof is named and counted: “1,000,000+ JARS SOLD,” “Rated 4.9 based on 3400+ reviews,” with individual products carrying 150 to 540-plus reviews each.8 Her catalog already spans the full ecosystem Reverse Cavity only aspires to build: toothpaste ($20.47), tooth powder ($20.47), mouthwash ($24.97 to $29.97), mints ($14.97), a kids line, supplements, courses, an affiliate program, wholesale, and a physical retail storefront, plus Amazon and third-party resellers.10 Her authority moat extends to six published books, including a trade title from BenBella and Simon and Schuster (ISBN 9781637746783, ~May 2025), and a press wall spanning Fox 5, NewsNation, Reader’s Digest, The Gabby Reece Show, and The Resetter Podcast.26

Three forensic catches expose her flagship. The Complete Remineralizing Bundle ($100.97, retailer id COMP-REMIN-BUNDLE), the closest analog to the Reverse Cavity kit, was marked “Out of stock / Sold out” at capture, so the highest-ticket kit-equivalent was unbuyable.14 Despite 3,400-plus sitewide reviews, that same bundle showed “Based on 1 review,” and the single review carried an “Incentivized review” disclosure, posted 12 hours before capture with a vendor reply 23 seconds later.15 Separately, independent lab tester Tamara Rubin (Lead Safe Mama) reported in February 2025 that a kids “Fresh Citrus Remineralizing Tooth Powder, formulated with Living Well with Dr. Michelle,” tested positive for “Very High Levels of Cadmium, Arsenic, and Lead,” a heavy-metals flag on a children’s product from a brand positioned as clean.21

Adjacent whitespace next to Jorgensen. Her store is a 14-category marketplace, so a buyer who wants one guided “reverse my cavities” protocol faces choice overload; a single decisive kit is a clean wedge.10 Her flagship bundle was out of stock, so an always-in-stock kit with simple subscription wins on availability.14 The heavy-metals flag on a kids product leaves the “actually clean, here are our published third-party purity results” high ground open for a competitor to take.21 A rep selling against Jorgensen names the out-of-stock flagship and the published lab finding, then offers one in-stock, purity-documented kit.

2.3 Bliss Energetics: the herbal house with a 30-herb formula and a soft proof base

Bliss Energetics runs blissenergetics.com, a San Diego Shopify store whose entire featured line is fluoride-free remineralization: Tooth Puddy ($40-$65), Super Mineral Toothpaste ($26), Ozonated Tooth Serum ($31), and Bone Renewal herbal blend ($53).11 The brand frames itself as an herbal-healing house in the Ayurvedic and Traditional Chinese Medicine traditions that happens to specialize in oral remineralization, with the hero line “All-natural, energetically-balanced herbal products crafted with love and intention” and the banner “Handcrafted with love in San Diego.”11 This is a meaningfully different framing from a biomedical “reverse cavities” pitch.

Bliss leads with ingredient superiority: “20% Micro-hydroxyapatite from fossilized coral, non-nano and used at twice the amount of most products on the market,” plus a proprietary formula of 30-plus herbs including shilajit, hadjod, drynaria, rehmannia, and theobromine.12 A 30-herb Ayurvedic deck is the opposite of an off-the-shelf bundle, so the repackaging charge does not reach Bliss at all. Its most durable Power is a distinctive brand identity, the energetic and TCM framing that is hard to copy, backed by 563 reviews at roughly 4.42 out of 5.12 It holds no scale economies and is explicitly anti-scale (“small batches made in the USA”), and its switching costs are mild (subscription plus a private Facebook group).

Bliss occupies nearly every position Reverse Cavity treats as unique. Its remineralization page reads “Remineralization is much more than just topical treatment! True remineralization comes from a combination of daily habits, targeted topicals, the right diet, and proper absorption of minerals. It is whole body healing!”4 It prescribes K2, D3, and trace minerals, runs a “Breaking Up with Fluoride” page, leans on a before-and-after photo gallery, and carries holistic-dentist endorsements in reviews (“Our holistic dentist recommended the tooth putty for my toddler”).4 18 27 It runs paid social, an affiliate program, and Faire wholesale, the same growth stack Reverse Cavity wants.28

The visible weaknesses sit in its own reviews and a forensic pass. Buyers report taste and texture rejection (“the taste and texture were not something I could manage,” with a full refund), no perceptible result (“in a week or 2, I cant really tell a difference”), adhesion and packaging friction, and a bee-allergy exclusion that narrows the market.29 The forensic pass found a navigation typo (“Reminerilizing”) in the slide-out menu while the mega-menu spells it correctly; an aggressive comparative claim (“twice the amount of most products on the market”) and a theobromine efficacy claim (“promising results compared to fluoride”) sitting directly above the FDA disclaimer; and several non-endorsement reviews scored 5/5 (one reads “How can I fairly review a product that I have only just recently purchased”) that inflate the 4.42 headline.30

Adjacent whitespace next to Bliss. Bliss sells modular products with no single guided kit, so a one-decision packaged protocol is a clearer experience.11 Its astrology and reiki framing can alienate the skeptical, evidence-first buyer, which leaves room for a clinically credible position built on X-rays and a documented founder case.11 Bliss is small-batch and not built for scaled DTC throughput, leaving the marketing-driven national lane open. A rep selling against Bliss leads with the documented dentist-confirmed case and the simple guided kit, not energetics.

3. Whitespace Map

Figure 2. Each dot is a competitor; dot size reflects market presence. Rivals cluster on credentials while the rust-shaded lower-right lane - fluoride-free, ingestible inside-out, single guided kit - is held by no credentialed rival.

A genuinely open lane has no named competitor in it. An apparently open lane carries the reason a competitor has stayed out, because that same reason often blocks Reverse Cavity too.

Genuinely open: the fluoride-free ingestible inside-out system. Phillips is the only named rival with national authority in this space, and she is fluoride-forward and exclusively topical; she sells no ingestible mineral protocol and argues against the anti-fluoride camp.6 14 Jorgensen and Bliss sell fluoride-free products but as catalogs or modular shelves, not as one guided ingestible-mineral protocol. The combination of fluoride-free plus ingestible plus single guided kit is held by no named competitor.

  • Genuinely open: the always-in-stock, single-decision guided kit. Jorgensen’s flagship bundle was out of stock at capture and her catalog forces choice overload; Bliss sells à-la-carte with no kit.14 10 11 A buyer who wants one protocol and a clear start has no clean option among the three.

Genuinely open: the international buyer. Phillips’s kit cannot ship outside the continental USA.6 A self-contained product can serve global demand the named leader explicitly turns away.

Apparently open: the “actually clean, here are our lab results” purity high ground. The heavy-metals lab finding on a Jorgensen-formulated kids product leaves this lane exposed.21 It is only apparently open for Reverse Cavity, because taking it requires the client to publish its own third-party heavy-metal and purity results, which the ledger does not confirm it has done. The lane is open to whoever publishes the COAs first. [client to confirm whether third-party purity testing exists]

Apparently open: the credentialed-authority lane. All three rivals hold credential or formulation authority that Reverse Cavity does not.7 16 11 This lane looks open only because the client lacks a clinician founder; the practical substitute is independent practitioner endorsement and a documented multi-customer cohort, neither of which the client holds today.5 Treat this as a build, not a grab.

4. TCO Comparison Logic

Sticker price understates what a buyer spends to actually run each protocol, and the comparison turns on add-ons, refill cadence, and whether the published kit is buyable. Reverse Cavity’s own pricing is not in the ledger, so the figures below are competitor-sourced only; the rep completes the comparison live using the worksheet in each battle card. No aggregate cross-brand cost figure is asserted here, because none is sourced.

  1. Phillips. The kit is $68, but it is a five-product bundle of grocery-store SKUs, so the buyer either keeps repurchasing CloSYS, Crest, Listerine, ACT, and Zellie’s separately or pays the $68 again, and the $199 course is a stacked upsell against a claimed “comparable value $3,000.”1 19 The real first-year spend for a committed buyer is the kit plus refills plus, in many funnels, the course. Subscribe and Save trims 15%, and free shipping requires a $75 order.
  2. Jorgensen. Single items run $20.47 each, so a buyer assembling the inside-out system (toothpaste plus powder plus mouthwash plus supplements) stacks several $20-$30 line items rather than paying one kit price.10 The flagship bundle that would consolidate them ($100.97) was out of stock at capture, so the consolidating discount was unavailable and the realistic spend is the à-la-carte sum.14 Free shipping requires a $99 order.
  3. Bliss. Tooth Puddy is $40 (1 oz) to $65 (2 oz), toothpaste $26, serum $31, with subscription saving roughly 5% and free US shipping over $75.11 A spot-treatment-plus-toothpaste-plus-serum starting set is a $90-plus first order before the herbal blend, and the modular structure pushes the buyer to add SKUs over time.

The TCO argument for Reverse Cavity is structural: one guided kit with one decision and one subscription is cheaper to reason about than a five-SKU bundle (Phillips), a multi-line-item catalog (Jorgensen), or an à-la-carte shelf (Bliss). The dollar comparison is completed per deal with the client’s own confirmed price. [client to confirm kit price and subscription terms]

5. Migration Friction Map and Anti-Switching Triggers

Switching cost in this category is low, because the products are consumable and there is no data lock-in. What keeps a buyer in place is habit, a credential they trust, or a subscription they have not cancelled. The triggers that start a buyer looking are a result that never arrives, a clean-label claim that fails an ingredient or lab check, and a fluoride decision that flips. Each cue below is a phrase a rep listens for, drawn from a real review verbatim.

  1. Switching from Phillips. Friction is the trust she earned with a DDS and a Huberman logo, plus the sunk cost of a $199 course. The opening is fluoride: her buyers who turn anti-fluoride discover the system runs on Crest and ACT. Listener cues, from real verbatims: a buyer who says the protocol made things worse (“my teeth have never felt so sensitive,” “tartar is forming on my front bottom teeth, I don’t think this is the secret sauce”); a buyer outside the continental USA who cannot buy the kit; and a buyer who has decided against fluoride and grocery-store brands.23 6
  2. Switching from Jorgensen. Friction is her credential stack and a catalog the buyer has already learned to navigate. The opening is availability and clean-label trust. Listener cues: a buyer who tried to buy the bundle and found it sold out; a buyer rattled by the heavy-metals report on a kids product; and a buyer overwhelmed by a 14-category store who wants one decision.14 21 10 A clean review verbatim that signals a switchable, results-driven Jorgensen buyer: “Before I found Living wells tooth paste, my teeth were falling apart,” which shows the buyer is chasing the strength result and will follow it to a clearer product.31
  3. Switching from Bliss. Friction is the distinctive herbal identity and a private community. The opening is the no-result experience and the energetics framing that loses evidence-first buyers. Listener cues: a buyer who says the putty fell off or was annoying to apply; a buyer who saw no difference (“in a week or 2, I cant really tell a difference”); and a skeptical buyer who wants clinical proof rather than astrology.29

Cross-incumbent cues (the natural-health buyer ready to switch from anyone). The fluoride-skeptical forum and review record names the buyer who is actively shopping: “the bigger part is that I personally don’t believe in the whole conventional dentistry story”; “Maybe I am now a cash cow for them”; and the cautionary “I wish I had crowned it right away and had not listened to the remineralization hype,” which signals a buyer burned by an over-promise and now wanting an honest claim.32 33 34 The Boka defector cue, “it’s actually not that great, not effective and the ingredients are not as good as they appear,” marks a buyer who bought on social proof and wants real proof next time.35

6. Compliance Posture

The category operates under active federal enforcement on exactly the claim Reverse Cavity’s name makes. FDA treats “remineralize / prevent cavities / heal / reverse tooth decay” as unapproved drug (disease) claims. In Warning Letter MARCS-CMS 684466 (November 21, 2024), FDA cited Dr. Brite, LLC for copy reading “Remineralize teeth naturally… Hydroxyapatite helps restore and strengthen the tooth enamel… while preventing cavities,” ruling the claims made hydroxyapatite an unapproved drug active ingredient; an earlier letter (MARCS-CMS 542508, March 30, 2018) cited Ozark Country Herbs for “proven to fill in cavities.”36 FTC guidance holds that consumer testimonials and before-and-after anecdotes are not “competent and reliable scientific evidence” for a disease-treatment claim, and that a supplement disclaimer “does not cure an otherwise deceptive ad.”37 This is the binding frame: a testimonial-plus-founder-case credibility model is exactly what FTC says is insufficient when a disease-level claim is implied.

Dental science bounds the truthful claim. Surface remineralization of early, non-cavitated white-spot lesions is well established. Once a lesion reaches cavitation or penetrates dentin, the damage is irreversible by natural or systemic means and requires mechanical restoration.38 No modern clinical trial shows that systemic vitamin D, K2, or calcium reverses established cavities; a 2025 K2 review calls such claims “premature.”39 The defensible ceiling for any brand in this category is arresting and remineralizing early decay and strengthening enamel, not regrowing a structural hole.

The competitors carry the same exposure and have not all cleared it. Bliss runs comparative and efficacy claims (“twice the amount of most products,” theobromine “promising results compared to fluoride”) directly above the FDA disclaimer.30 Jorgensen states “Better than fluoride for remineralization.”3 Phillips claims her system “helps heal cavities.”2 None has been shown in the ledger to hold the clinical substantiation FTC would require for a disease claim. Reverse Cavity’s literal “reverse your cavities” hero is the most exposed copy of the four.

Auditable-specifics checklist. A two-column punch list of what the client can claim today versus what the named natural-health buyer (and a regulator) will look for.

Shipped / defensible today The ICP and regulator will look for, still needed
A founder before-and-after with an independent third dentist on the record5 Independent practitioner endorsement beyond the founder’s own case [client to confirm]
Self-reported 2,000+ protocol users (published third-party)40 A documented multi-customer outcomes cohort, not a self-reported count [client to confirm]
Fluoride-free clean-label positioning Published third-party heavy-metal / purity COAs for the kit [client to confirm]
Customer testimonials and the viral video A legal review of the defensible claim ceiling before the next campaign [client to confirm]
The inside-out mechanism narrative (category-standard) Copy aligned to “arrest and remineralize early decay, strengthen enamel” rather than the unqualified “reverse”38 39

7. Vulnerability Watch

Lead with the live-page forensic catches, because a craft tell on a paid checkout page is the cheapest exposure to exploit and the hardest for a rival to deny.

7.1 Phillips’s $199 course page ships unfinished

The Vulnerability. A paid checkout page that still renders template placeholder text and a typo signals the offer was rushed and the buyer is paying premium for unfinished craft. The Evidence. The bottom of learn.drellie.com renders “What if… Awesome Hook Headline / Benefit Driven Sub Headline Here / Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet,” and a headline on the same page reads “The Complete Mouth Care Syste(TM) is the steps” (missing the “m”).22 The Window. Open now, and it closes the moment Phillips runs a basic QA pass on her Kajabi build, which is a small fix. Recommended Response. A rep fielding a Phillips comparison says: “Her $199 program still has placeholder Lorem-ipsum text live on the sales page. We ship one finished, in-stock kit and a guided protocol; you are paying for a complete product, not a course with an unfinished page.”

7.2 Jorgensen’s flagship kit was unbuyable and unproven at capture

The Vulnerability. The product closest to a guided “reverse my cavities” kit was both out of stock and resting on a single incentivized review, so her strongest analog to the Reverse Cavity kit had no availability and no independent validation. The Evidence. The Complete Remineralizing Bundle ($100.97, COMP-REMIN-BUNDLE) showed “Out of stock / Sold out,” and the same page showed “Based on 1 review,” that review flagged “Incentivized review,” posted 12 hours before capture with a vendor reply 23 seconds later.14 15 The Window. The stock gap closes whenever she restocks; the proof gap closes only as real reviews accumulate, which takes time. Recommended Response. A rep says: “Her complete bundle was sold out, and the one review on it was an incentivized review posted hours earlier. Our kit is in stock and the proof is a founder reversal a third independent dentist confirmed on his X-rays.”

7.3 A heavy-metals lab finding on a Jorgensen-formulated kids product

The Vulnerability. A brand built on “clean” and “safe enough for her own family” carries a published independent lab report flagging high cadmium, arsenic, and lead in a children’s product, which is the precise opposite of its promise. The Evidence. Tamara Rubin (Lead Safe Mama), February 2025, reported a kids “Fresh Citrus Remineralizing Tooth Powder, formulated with Living Well with Dr. Michelle,” positive for “Very High Levels of Cadmium, Arsenic, and Lead.”21 The Window. Open while the report stands online; it informs the purity high ground only if Reverse Cavity publishes its own third-party COAs. Recommended Response. A rep says, factually and without overclaiming: “An independent lab reported high heavy metals in a kids tooth powder she formulated. If purity is your concern, ask any brand for its third-party metals testing.” (The client should be able to produce its own COAs before leaning on this line.) [client to confirm]

7.4 Bliss runs unsubstantiated comparative claims above its own disclaimer

The Vulnerability. Aggressive comparative and efficacy claims sit directly above the FDA disclaimer, which is the regulatory exposure pattern FDA warning letters target. The Evidence. “20% Micro-hydroxyapatite… used at twice the amount of most products on the market” and theobromine “promising results compared to fluoride” appear directly above “not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease,” and a navigation typo renders the category “Reminerilizing.”30 The Window. Open until Bliss substantiates or removes the comparative claims. Recommended Response. A rep keeps it about standard, not attack: “Some brands claim ‘twice the hydroxyapatite of competitors’ without published backing, sitting right above the disclaimer. We make claims we can defend and keep our copy inside what the science supports.”

7.5 Phillips’s “natural” system runs on grocery-store fluoride products

The Vulnerability. The clean-living buyer who chose Phillips for a “natural” system is, on inspection, brushing with Crest and rinsing with ACT and Listerine, and Phillips publicly defends fluoride against that buyer’s own beliefs. The Evidence. Her $68 kit contains Crest Cavity Protection, Listerine, and ACT sodium-fluoride rinse, described by her own copy as commonly found in grocery stores, and she defends fluoride on TikTok (“If you’re so against fluoride, why aren’t you giving up these foods then?”).1 14 The Window. Durable, because reversing it would require Phillips to abandon her stated pro-fluoride position. Recommended Response. A rep says: “Her natural system is Crest, Listerine, and ACT in a box, and she defends fluoride publicly. If you want fluoride-free, hers is not it; ours is fluoride-free and built as one ingestible protocol.”

8. Cliche Saturation Index

A phrase appears below when three or more named competitors use it, each occurrence sourced. The two highlighted rows are the language Reverse Cavity must stop treating as a sole differentiator, because the very competitors it claims to beat already own it.

Phrase Competitors who use it Recommendation for Reverse Cavity
“Inside-out / from the inside out” Phillips2, Jorgensen3, Bliss4 Must retire as sole differentiator; keep only as shared category context
“Remineralize / remineralization from within” Phillips2, Jorgensen3, Bliss4 Must retire as sole differentiator
“Works with / in harmony with your body” Phillips2, Jorgensen3, Bliss4 Differentiate; the phrase is table stakes, the proof behind it is not
“Root cause / lack of internal nourishment” Jorgensen25, Bliss4, plus category13 Differentiate; lead with the documented case, not the slogan
“Fluoride-free” Jorgensen9, Bliss18 (Phillips is pro-fluoride) Acceptable as an audience signal; two rivals share it, so it qualifies the buyer rather than differentiates the brand
“Heal / reverse cavities” Phillips2, Jorgensen, Bliss (category)13 Differentiate and constrain; this is the regulated claim, sharpen to “arrest and remineralize early decay”38
Before-and-after photos + testimonials as proof Phillips7, Jorgensen8, Bliss12 Differentiate; the independent dentist confirmation is the proof rivals lack5

9. Battle Cards

Each Defense Line ships in a short version for a live call and a long version for a follow-up email; only the rep’s spoken words appear, and the supporting fact behind each maps to a sourced competitor fact.

9.1 Battle Card: Dr. Ellie Phillips

30-Second Positioning Summary. Phillips is a credentialed DDS with tier-one media reach selling a $68 box of Crest, Listerine, and ACT plus her own xylitol, differentiated by a six-step timing ritual. She is fluoride-forward, topical only, and her kit ships only inside the continental USA. We win with a fluoride-free ingestible protocol she does not offer and a buyer she actively argues against.

Top 3 Attack Lines.

  • “Her ‘natural’ system is Crest, Listerine, and ACT in a box; she says so herself.”
  • “She defends fluoride publicly, so if you are fluoride-free, she is on the other side of that line.”
  • “Her kit cannot ship outside the continental USA, and her $199 course page still has placeholder text live.”

Top 3 Defense Lines.

  • “How can you compete with a DDS and a Huberman endorsement?” Short: “We do not out-credential her; we out-prove the result, with a reversal a third independent dentist confirmed on the X-rays.” Long: “Her credential is real and we respect it. The question for you is proof of the result, and ours is a documented before-and-after that an independent dentist confirmed showed remineralization, not a logo.” (Maps to CMP-013, CUS-026.)
  • “She’s been doing this since 1970, you’re new.” Short: “Decades of selling grocery-store products in a box is tenure, not a different mechanism; ours is one fluoride-free ingestible protocol she does not sell.” Long: “She has been bundling Crest and ACT for years, which is exactly the repackaging you are trying to get away from. We are fluoride-free and built as one ingestible system, which is a category she does not operate in.” (Maps to CMP-010, CMP-017.)
  • “Her reviews are huge, 1,511 of them.” Short: “Volume of reviews on a fluoride bundle is not proof for a fluoride-free buyer; her own reviews include people whose teeth got worse.” Long: “She has volume because she has reach. Her own kit reviews include buyers reporting new sensitivity and tartar, and the products are fluoride. For a fluoride-free buyer the relevant proof is a documented reversal, which is what we lead with.” (Maps to CMP-016.)

Key Proof Points. Her own copy calls the kit “products commonly found in grocery stores.”1 Her kit contains Crest, Listerine, and ACT fluoride.1 Her $199 course page renders live “Lorem ipsum” placeholder text.22

Case math for this deal. Read aloud: a third independent dentist reviewing the founder’s before-and-after X-rays said “There is definite remineralization in these second x-rays.”5 TCO worksheet for the rep to fill live: Phillips kit $68 + expected refills of CloSYS/Crest/Listerine/ACT over [buyer’s timeframe] + the $199 course if upsold, versus Reverse Cavity [client to confirm kit price] + [subscription] for one fluoride-free protocol.

9.2 Battle Card: Dr. Michelle Jorgensen

30-Second Positioning Summary. Jorgensen is a credentialed DDS and naturopath with a 14-category catalog, six books, and 3,400-plus reviews. Her authority is real and her breadth is the ecosystem we are still building. Her weak points are operational: the flagship kit was out of stock with one incentivized review, and an independent lab flagged heavy metals in a kids product she formulated. We win on focus, availability, and an honest, documented case.

Top 3 Attack Lines.

  • “Her complete bundle was sold out, and the one review on it was incentivized and posted hours before.”
  • “An independent lab reported high cadmium, arsenic, and lead in a kids powder she formulated.”
  • “Fourteen product categories is a lot to navigate when you want one decision and one protocol.”

Top 3 Defense Lines.

  • “She’s a real dentist and a naturopath, why would I trust a non-dentist brand?” Short: “A credential is who made it; the question is whether it works, and ours is a reversal an independent dentist documented.” Long: “Her credentials are genuine. What you are buying is a result, and the proof that matters is independent: a third dentist confirmed remineralization on the founder’s X-rays. We point you to the documented case, not the title.” (Maps to CMP-023, CUS-026.)
  • “She’s sold a million jars and has 3,400 reviews.” Short: “Sitewide reviews are not kit reviews; her flagship bundle showed a single incentivized review.” Long: “The sitewide number is large, but the bundle closest to our kit showed ‘Based on 1 review,’ and that one was flagged incentivized. Volume across a catalog does not validate the specific protocol you would buy.” (Maps to CMP-019, CMP-021.)
  • “She already sells everything, supplements, toothpaste, kids products.” Short: “Breadth is choice overload when you want one guided protocol, and her flagship bundle was out of stock.” Long: “A 14-category store makes you assemble your own system, and the bundle that would consolidate it was sold out at the time we checked. We sell one in-stock guided kit so you make one decision.” (Maps to CMP-024, CMP-020.)

Key Proof Points. Her flagship Complete Remineralizing Bundle was marked “Out of stock.”14 That bundle showed “Based on 1 review,” flagged incentivized.15 Lead Safe Mama reported high cadmium, arsenic, and lead in a kids powder she formulated (Feb 2025).21

Case math for this deal. Read aloud: customers report the result Reverse Cavity sells, including a homepage testimonial where a buyer’s dentist “opened up my mouth, checked everywhere, did the test and said, I don’t see any cavities.”41 TCO worksheet: Jorgensen à-la-carte (toothpaste $20.47 + powder $20.47 + mouthwash $24.97-$29.97 + supplements) summed for [buyer’s regimen], noting the $100.97 consolidating bundle was unavailable, versus Reverse Cavity [client to confirm kit price] for one guided kit.10 14

9.3 Battle Card: Bliss Energetics

30-Second Positioning Summary. Bliss is a small-batch San Diego herbal house with a 30-herb proprietary formula, 563 reviews around 4.42, and a distinctive energetic and Ayurvedic identity. It is à-la-carte with no guided kit, leans astrology and reiki, and runs comparative claims above its disclaimer. We win with one guided clinically framed kit and a documented case for the evidence-first buyer.

Top 3 Attack Lines.

  • “It is sold à-la-carte; there is no single guided protocol, you assemble it yourself.”
  • “Its branding leans astrology and energetics, which is not for everyone wanting clinical proof.”
  • “It claims ‘twice the hydroxyapatite of most products’ with no published backing, right above the FDA disclaimer.”

Top 3 Defense Lines. (varied opener: objections framed as buyer worries)

  • Buyer worry: “Their formula has 30 herbs, isn’t that more sophisticated?” Short: “A long herbal list is a recipe, not a result; what we show you is a documented reversal.” Long: “A 30-herb deck sounds impressive and it is genuinely not off-the-shelf. The buying question is evidence of the outcome, and ours is an independent dentist confirming remineralization on X-rays, which a herb list does not give you.” (Maps to CMP-003, CUS-026.)
  • Buyer worry: “They have 563 reviews at 4.4 stars.” Short: “Some of those 5-star reviews are non-endorsements that inflate the average, and several buyers report no result.” Long: “Their headline rating includes 5-star reviews that openly say the buyer cannot yet judge the product, and others report no change in a couple of weeks. We would rather show you one documented, dentist-confirmed case than a padded average.” (Maps to CMP-008, CMP-006.)
  • Buyer worry: “The energetic, all-natural story really appeals to me.” Short: “We are fully natural and fluoride-free too, with a clinically documented result instead of astrology.” Long: “If natural and fluoride-free is what you want, we are both. The difference is we anchor the promise in a documented before-and-after a dentist confirmed, rather than reiki and the astrology of teeth, so the evidence-first part of you is covered too.” (Maps to CMP-002, CMP-001.)

Key Proof Points. Tooth Puddy shows 563 reviews at ~4.42, with non-endorsement 5/5 reviews inflating it.12 30 Its comparative claim sits above the FDA disclaimer.30 It is sold à-la-carte with no single kit.11

Case math for this deal. Read aloud: a buyer following the guided protocol reported “about ten days in… there was an overall increased feeling of strength in my teeth.”24 TCO worksheet: Bliss starter set (Tooth Puddy $40-$65 + toothpaste $26 + serum $31) summed for [buyer’s regimen], versus Reverse Cavity [client to confirm kit price] for one guided protocol.11

10. Strategic Implications

Each implication below acts on a finding in this report and reinforces a recommendation in the Strategic Alignment Brief.

Retire “we have the unique inside-out mechanism” as the lead and re-anchor on proof and guidance. Phillips, Jorgensen, and Bliss all publish the inside-out, works-with-your-body, root-cause language verbatim, so the mechanism is table stakes.2 3 4 13 Lead instead with the founder’s independently dentist-confirmed reversal and the guided complete-protocol kit, and demote the mechanism to shared category context.5

  • Commission independent, transferable proof before scaling spend. The defensible proof today is a single documented case plus a self-reported user count; rivals hold credentials and review volume the client cannot match.5 40 7 16 Commission an independent practitioner-supervised before-and-after X-ray cohort to convert the founder’s n=1 into transferable proof.

Sharpen the “reverse your cavities” hero to the defensible claim and run a legal review. FDA cites this exact claim type as an unapproved drug claim and FTC rejects testimonials as substantiation for it.36 37 Rewrite every public surface to claim arresting and remineralizing early decay and strengthening enamel, and commission a legal claim-ceiling review before the next campaign.38

Claim the fluoride-free ingestible inside-out lane Phillips cedes. Phillips is fluoride-forward, topical only, and continental-USA only, so the fluoride-free ingestible single-protocol position is open.6 14 Anchor the brand on that combination and target the fluoride-skeptical buyer she argues against.

Win on availability and focus against Jorgensen, then expand the catalog. Her flagship kit was out of stock with one incentivized review and her catalog forces choice overload.14 15 10 Hold one always-in-stock single-decision kit as the spine and defer the toothpaste-toothbrush-affiliate expansion until the beachhead proves unit economics.

Publish third-party purity results to take the clean high ground. A heavy-metals lab finding on a Jorgensen-formulated kids product leaves the verifiable-purity lane exposed.21 Commission and publish third-party heavy-metal and purity COAs for the kit, then lead the clean-label claim with them. [client to confirm testing exists]

Diversify beyond the single viral asset. The viral video drew ~480.6K verifiable views (not the claimed 1-2M), single-channel concentration is a documented existential risk, and self-reported revenue swung sharply with founder availability.42 43 Stand up a deliberate multi-channel plan and test whether a second produced proof asset generates traffic without luck.

Sequence a specific reachable beachhead before the global ambition. The serviceable remineralizing niche is low-single-billions, the “everyone gets cavities” figure concentrates in populations least able to buy a premium kit, and Jorgensen already owns the full-catalog ecosystem.44 45 10 Concentrate the proof-and-purpose spine on a specific natural-health and faith-conscious community and prove the economics there first.

Sources

  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, Complete Mouth Care System page, drellie.com/complete-mouth-care-system/ (kit contents and “commonly found in grocery stores”).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, Complete Mouth Care System page, drellie.com/complete-mouth-care-system/ (“works in harmony with your body,” “natural mineralization… helps heal cavities”).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, Complete Remineralizing Bundle page, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/products/ultimate-remineralizing-bundle (“Support Your Teeth From the Inside Out”).
  • Bliss Energetics, Fundamentals of Remineralization page, blissenergetics.com/pages/fundamentals-of-remineralization (“It is whole body healing!”).
  • Independent dentist quote on Gabriel Arruda’s before/after X-rays, “How I Reversed 5 Cavities in 3 Months,” Life & Health Network, Mar 4 2026 (“There is definite remineralization in these second x-rays”).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, Complete Mouth Care System page / kit page, drellie.com; shop.zellies.com (topical-only system; continental-USA-only shipping).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, course and store pages, learn.drellie.com / shop.zellies.com (DDS 1970, AAOSH, Huberman endorsement, media logos; store-wide 1,511 reviews).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, homepage, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com (“1,000,000+ JARS SOLD,” “Rated 4.9 based on 3400+ reviews”).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, homepage, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com (“Fluoride-Free Dental Care Made by a Dentist Who Refused to Compromise”).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, store catalog and product pages, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com (toothpaste $20.47, tooth powder $20.47, mouthwash $24.97-$29.97, mints $14.97, kids line, supplements, courses, storefront).
  • Bliss Energetics, homepage and product pages, blissenergetics.com (positioning, product line and prices, à-la-carte structure).
  • Bliss Energetics, Tooth Puddy product page, blissenergetics.com/products/remineralizing-tooth-putty-spot-treatment (“Overall rating: 4.419183 / 5 from 563 reviews”).
  • Cliche-saturation sources: davids-usa.com; greatoralhealth.com; elementalwellnessshop.com; livingwellwithdrmichelle.com (product pages, 2025).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, Complete Remineralizing Bundle page, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/products/ultimate-remineralizing-bundle (“Out of stock / Sold out,” $100.97).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, Complete Remineralizing Bundle page, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/products/ultimate-remineralizing-bundle (“Based on 1 review,” incentivized-review disclosure).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, About page, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/pages/about (DDS + Board Certified Traditional Naturopath, ~17 yrs practice, “Total Care Way”).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, Complete Mouth Care System page, drellie.com/complete-mouth-care-system/ (H1 and system promise).
  • Bliss Energetics, “Breaking Up with Fluoride” / Learning Center, blissenergetics.com (fluoride-free counter-positioning).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, kit and course pages, shop.zellies.com/products/dr-ellies-complete-mouth-care-system-kit; learn.drellie.com ($68 kit, $199 course vs. “comparable value $3,000”; kit 180 reviews).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, course FAQ, learn.drellie.com (repeat-sufferer ICP, “why hasn’t my dentist told me about this”).
  • Tamara Rubin (Lead Safe Mama), Feb 2025 lab report, tamararubin.com (kids “Fresh Citrus Remineralizing Tooth Powder… Formulated with Living Well with Dr. Michelle,” “Very High Levels of Cadmium, Arsenic, and Lead”).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, course sales page, learn.drellie.com (live Kajabi placeholder “Awesome Hook Headline / Lorem ipsum”; “The Complete Mouth Care Syste(TM)” typo).
  • Dr. Ellie Phillips, kit page reviews, shop.zellies.com/products/dr-ellies-complete-mouth-care-system-kit (Katy S.; “Hopeful” 4-star).
  • Reverse Cavity Protocol Kit landing page testimonial by Nathaniel, reverseautoimmune.com (“ten days in… increased feeling of strength”).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, Complete Remineralizing Bundle page, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/products/ultimate-remineralizing-bundle (“to support the body’s natural ability to heal,” “lack of internal nourishment”).
  • Simon & Schuster, “Living Well with Dr. Michelle,” ISBN 9781637746783, ~May 2025; homepage press wall, livingwellwithdrmichelle.com.
  • Bliss Energetics, Tooth Puddy product page, blissenergetics.com/products/remineralizing-tooth-putty-spot-treatment (holistic-dentist endorsement, child use).
  • Bliss Energetics, affiliate registration page, blissenergetics.com/pages/affiliate-registration (affiliate program, Faire wholesale, paid social).
  • Bliss Energetics, Tooth Puddy product page reviews, blissenergetics.com/products/remineralizing-tooth-putty-spot-treatment (taste/texture, “in a week or 2, I cant really tell a difference,” packaging, bee-allergy exclusion).
  • Bliss Energetics, about and product pages, blissenergetics.com/pages/about-us (forensic: “Reminerilizing” typo; “twice the amount of most products” above FDA disclaimer; non-endorsement 5/5 reviews).
  • Dr. Michelle Jorgensen, Remineralizing Toothpaste review by Roland W., livingwellwithdrmichelle.com/products/remineralizing-toothpaste-1-year-supply (“my teeth were falling apart… now my teeth are sooo much Stronger”).
  • Permies forum, “Tooth remineralization quandary,” by Thekla McDaniels, permies.com/t/202894 (“I personally don’t believe in the whole conventional dentistry story”).
  • Permies forum, “Tooth remineralization quandary,” by Thekla McDaniels, permies.com/t/202894 (“Maybe I am now a cash cow for them”).
  • Permies forum, “Tooth remineralization quandary,” forum member, permies.com/t/202894 (“I wish I had crowned it right away and had not listened to the remineralization hype”).
  • Thingtesting, Boka reviews, by Willy H, thingtesting.com/brands/boka/reviews (“not that great, not effective and the ingredients are not as good as they appear”).
  • FDA Warning Letters MARCS-CMS 684466 (2024-11-21, Dr. Brite, LLC) and 542508 (2018-03-30, Ozark Country Herbs), fda.gov.
  • FTC Health Products Compliance Guidance, 2022/2023; FTC Dietary Supplements Advertising Guide, ftc.gov.
  • Advanced non-fluoride approaches to enamel remineralization, PMC9934497 (surface remineralization vs. irreversible cavitation).
  • Nutrients narrative review, May 2024; Vitamin K2 dental review, Oct 2025 (no clinical evidence K2 reverses established cavities).
  • Gabriel Arruda, “How I Reversed 5 Cavities in 3 Months,” Life & Health Network, Mar 4 2026 (“over 2,000 people” protocol count).
  • Reverse Cavity homepage testimonial, reversecavity.com, “Does it work?” section (“I don’t see any cavities”).
  • YouTube, “The Treatment for Tooth Decay that Reversed my 5 Cavities in 3 Months!!” (id XrpNrJoH4wI), ~480.6K views as of June 2026 (founder claimed ~1-2M).
  • “The DTC Graveyard: 50 Consumer Brand Failures,” PR Newswire, 2026 (single-channel concentration as existential risk).
  • Grand View Research, Oral Care Market, 2025; Dataintelo, Nano Hydroxyapatite Toothpaste Market, 2024 (serviceable niche low-single-billions).
  • WHO Oral Health Fact Sheet, 2022/2023; Lancet GBD 2021 oral conditions analysis (clinical-burden figure concentrated in low-income populations).
Reverse Cavity
